1.1: In the eighth month of Darius's second year, Yahweh's word came to the prophet Zechariah son of Berechiah son of Iddo.
1.2: Yahweh burned with anger against your ancestors — this is where you come from.
1.3: So tell them: This is the word of Yahweh the Almighty — Come back to me, and I will come back to you. The door moves from both sides.
1.4: Don't repeat your ancestors' mistake. The earlier prophets called out: Yahweh commands — turn from your evil paths and your wicked deeds. They refused to listen; they refused to pay attention. Don't be them.
1.5: Your ancestors are gone. The prophets who warned them are gone. What remains is Yahweh's word — and what it did to them.
1.6: But my words and my decrees — given through the prophets, my servants — those ran your ancestors down and caught them. In the end they admitted it: Yahweh the Almighty carried out exactly what he promised, deed for deed, according to what we had done.
1.7: On the twenty-fourth of Shebat — three months into Darius's second year — Yahweh's word came again to Zechariah son of Berechiah son of Iddo.
1.8: In the night vision a rider appeared on a red horse, standing still among myrtle trees in a shadowed ravine. Behind him, more horses — red, sorrel, white. The whole earth is under surveillance.
1.9: I asked the angel beside me, What are all these? He said, Let me show you.
1.10: The rider among the myrtles answered: these are Yahweh's scouts — sent to patrol the whole earth.
1.11: The patrol returned their report to the angel of the LORD standing in the myrtles: We have gone through the whole earth — and it is still, it is settled. Not a nation trembles.
1.12: Then the angel of the LORD prayed aloud: How long, Yahweh of Heaven's Armies? Seventy years of your anger against Jerusalem and Judah's cities — how long until you show mercy?
1.13: Yahweh answered — and the words were kind, words of comfort.
1.14: The angel said to me, Announce this: Yahweh the Almighty declares — I am consumed with jealousy for Jerusalem, for Zion. My love for this city is as fierce as fire.
1.15: As much as I burn for Zion, I burn with fury against the nations who sat at ease. I let them serve as my instrument — but they went far beyond my anger. They made my judgment into a massacre.
1.16: So hear Yahweh's word: I have come back to Jerusalem with compassion. The temple will be built — Yahweh of Heaven's Armies promises it. And the surveyor's line is already being stretched across Jerusalem; it is a city being rebuilt.
1.17: Proclaim it again: Yahweh the Almighty says — my cities will spill over with good things. The LORD will comfort Zion again; he will choose Jerusalem again, as he always has.
1.19: I asked the angel beside me, What are these? He answered: the four powers that shattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem — scattered them to the winds.
1.20: Then Yahweh showed me something else: four smiths.
1.21: I asked, What are they here to do? He said: These smiths have come to break the horns — the powers that shattered Judah so completely no one could even lift their head. Those nations raised their horns against Yahweh's land; now comes the counter-blow.

2.1: I looked up: a man holding a surveyor's line.
2.2: I asked where he was going. He said: to measure Jerusalem — to calculate its width and its length.
2.3: Then the angel who was with me stepped forward — and another angel came out to intercept him.
2.4: The second angel said: Run, tell him — Jerusalem will burst its seams. So many people, so many animals, it will spread like open country with no walls to hold it.
2.5: Yahweh himself declares it: I will be her wall — a wall of fire circling her. And my glory will live inside her. No stone rampart is needed when the living God is the city's defense.
2.6: Up! Up! Get out of the north — now! says Yahweh. I scattered you like seed to the four winds of heaven; now I am gathering. Do not stay where I have called you out from.
2.7: Escape, all you who live in Babylon's shadow — come home to Zion!
2.8: Yahweh the Almighty speaks through the messenger: I was sent — after the glory — against the nations that plundered you. Here is the measure of how much he cares: to touch Israel is to jab a finger in Yahweh's own eye.
2.9: Watch: I am lifting my hand against those nations. The ones they enslaved will plunder them. And when it happens, you will know that Yahweh the Almighty sent me.
2.10: Sing, Zion — shout for joy! Yahweh is coming to live among you. This is the promise of the living God.
2.11: On that day many nations will attach themselves to Yahweh and become his people. He will live among you, and you will know beyond doubt that Yahweh the Almighty sent me. The covenant once made with Israel will expand to encompass the nations.
2.12: Yahweh will claim Judah as his own inheritance in the holy land — and Jerusalem will be chosen again, as it was chosen before.
2.13: Hush — all humanity — before Yahweh. He is awake. He has risen from his holy dwelling place and is on the move.

3.1: Then the vision shifted: Joshua the high priest stood before the angel of the LORD — and Satan stood at his right hand, ready to press charges.
3.2: But Yahweh spoke directly to Satan: Yahweh rebukes you, Satan. Yahweh, who has chosen Jerusalem, rebukes you. Look at this man — he is a burning brand just pulled from the fire. You do not get to prosecute what I have rescued.
3.3: Joshua stood there in filthy clothes — the accumulated filth of exile, of priestly defilement, of a people who had failed.
3.4: The angel commanded those attending: Strip off those filthy clothes. Then, turning to Joshua: Your guilt has been removed. I am clothing you in clean garments. The old is gone.
3.5: I called out myself: Put a clean turban on his head! So they placed the clean turban on him and clothed him fully — while the angel of the LORD stood there watching it all done.
3.6: Then the angel of the LORD spoke formally to Joshua — this was a charge, not a comfort:
3.7: Yahweh the Almighty speaks: Joshua, if you walk in my ways and keep my charge — then I give you the oversight of my house, authority in my courts, and the right to move freely among the angels who stand in my presence. Obedience is the condition; access is the promise.
3.8: Listen, Joshua the high priest — you and all the priests sitting with you. You are men of sign, living prophecy. Here is what you are a sign of: I am about to bring my servant — the Branch.
3.9: See this stone I have placed before Joshua — one stone, and on it seven eyes watching everything. I am engraving it myself, Yahweh the Almighty declares, and on a single day I will wipe out the iniquity of this land entirely. One act. One day. Everything clean.
3.10: When that day comes — Yahweh the Almighty promises it — neighbors will call to neighbors: Come, sit under my vine. Rest under my fig tree. It is the oldest image of peace, and it will be real.

4.1: The angel came back and woke me — shaking me like someone startled out of a deep sleep.
4.2: He asked what I saw. I told him: a lampstand, solid gold, with a bowl at its crown. Seven lamps on it, and seven channels running oil to each lamp. A design made for endless, sustained light.
4.3: Two olive trees stood beside it — one on the right of the bowl, one on the left. Living sources of oil, feeding the light continuously.
4.4: I turned to the angel: What does this all mean?
4.5: He answered my question with a question: Do you not know what these are? I admitted: No, my lord.
4.6: He said: This is Yahweh's word for Zerubbabel. Not by military force. Not by human power. Only by my Spirit — says Yahweh the Almighty. The temple will be built not by what Zerubbabel can marshal, but by what God supplies.
4.7: Every obstacle looming over this work — every great mountain of opposition — will become flat ground before Zerubbabel. And when he sets the capstone in place, the crowd will cry out: Grace! Grace! Yahweh's unearned favor carried this to completion.
4.8: Then Yahweh's word came to me a second time:
4.9: Zerubbabel's hands laid the foundation — and Zerubbabel's hands will set the final stone. Same person, start to finish. When it stands complete, you will know that Yahweh the Almighty sent me to you with this word.
4.10: Who dares to despise this small beginning? Those who mocked will be the ones who rejoice when they see Zerubbabel with the plumb line in his hand — the work is real. The seven lamps are the seven eyes of Yahweh, watching everything on earth. Nothing is hidden from him; no small thing escapes his notice.
4.11: I pressed him again: What are the two olive trees — one on the lampstand's right, one on its left?
4.12: I asked it more precisely: What are these two olive branches that channel golden oil through the golden pipes? Where does the supply come from, and what does it mean?
4.13: He asked again: Do you not know? And again I had to say: No, my lord. I do not.
4.14: He said: These are the two anointed ones — the two who stand beside the Lord of all the earth. They are the living conduit between the divine source and the lampstand's light. Priest and king, feeding the light that illumines the world.

5.1: I looked up: a scroll flying.
5.2: He asked what I saw. I told him: a scroll, in flight — thirty feet long and fifteen feet wide. Large enough to be read. Large enough to cover everything.
5.3: He said: This is the curse flying over the whole land. One side covers theft; the other covers false oaths. The scroll is the Torah's covenant curse — Deuteronomy 28 made visible and mobile — hunting down every violator in the land.
5.4: Yahweh the Almighty declares: I am sending this curse. It will enter the thief's house; it will enter the house of anyone who swears falsely by my name. It will settle into the walls and consume everything — wood and stone, roof and foundation. Nothing will be left.
5.5: The angel stepped forward: Look up. Something else is coming out.
5.6: I asked: What is it? He said: An ephah — a measuring basket. And then: This is their iniquity, the accumulated wickedness of the whole land, gathered and concentrated.
5.7: The lead cover was lifted — and there was a woman sitting inside the ephah.
5.8: He said simply: This is Wickedness. Then he shoved her back down and slammed the lead cover shut. Wickedness is real, it is personal, and it is being contained.
5.9: I looked up: two women, wings like a stork's, and the wind filling their wings. They lifted the basket up between earth and sky — carrying wickedness away.
5.10: I asked the angel: Where are they taking it?
5.11: He said: To Shinar — Babylon — where they will build it a shrine. Wickedness belongs in Babylon; it will be housed there, on its own pedestal, in the land where idolatry was born. It is being removed from Yahweh's land.

6.1: I looked up: four chariots emerging from between two bronze mountains. The image is cosmic — Yahweh's court dispatching his agents into every quarter of the world.
6.2: Red horses pulled the first chariot; black horses the second.
6.3: White horses pulled the third; dappled horses — strong, striking — pulled the fourth.
6.4: I asked the angel: What are these?
6.5: The angel answered: These are the four celestial spirits of heaven — Yahweh's agents going out into every quarter of the earth after standing in his presence. The whole earth is under his governance.
6.6: The black horses go north — toward Babylon, the great threat. The white follow after them. The dappled drive south. Each direction covered, each power watched.
6.7: The strong horses strained forward, impatient to be sent. He commanded: Go — patrol the earth. And they went, covering every corner.
6.8: Then he called to me: Look — the ones who went north have settled my Spirit there. Yahweh's wrath toward the north has been satisfied. Babylon's power is broken; his anger has found its rest.
6.9: Then Yahweh's word came to me directly:
6.10: Take gifts from the delegation just arrived from Babylon — Heldai, Tobijah, Jedaiah — and go that same day to Josiah son of Zephaniah's house.
6.11: From their silver and gold, make crowns. Place them on the head of Joshua son of Jehozadak — the high priest. This is a prophetic act: a priest wearing a crown.
6.12: And speak this word to him: Yahweh the Almighty says — Here is the man. His name is the Branch. He will grow up from where he is planted and build Yahweh's temple. What Zerubbabel has begun, the Branch will complete and perfect.
6.13: He will build the temple — and he will bear royal majesty. He will sit on his throne and rule. But he will also be a priest on that throne. Two roles in one person. The counsel of peace will flow between king and priest — because they are the same. This is the promise of Psalm 110 made visible: priest and king, Melchizedek's successor, at last.
6.14: The crowns will be hung in Yahweh's temple — a lasting reminder of Helem, Tobijah, Jedaiah, and Hen son of Zephaniah, and of what was spoken and enacted on this day.
6.15: People from distant lands will come and help build Yahweh's temple. And when they do, you will know that Yahweh the Almighty truly sent me with these words. But this is conditional: all of it comes to pass only if you faithfully obey the voice of Yahweh your God. The promise is sure; the door of obedience must be walked through.

13.1: On that day a spring will break open — a fountain flowing for the house of David and for every inhabitant of Jerusalem — to wash away sin and ritual defilement. The cleansing they could never achieve through sacrifice will flow freely from God's own hand, answering at last the mourning of 12:10.
13.2: That day arrives, and Yahweh of Armies declares: I will erase the idols from the land — even their names will be forgotten. With them I will sweep out the false prophets and the unclean spirit that animated their words. The land will be purged from the inside: the corrupted worship, the corrupted spokespersons, and the corrupted spiritual power that drove them, all removed together.
13.3: Should anyone still stand up to prophesy falsely, even his own parents will refuse him mercy. They will say: 'You are lying in the name of Yahweh. You cannot live.' And when he prophesies, it will be his own father and mother who plunge the sword into him. So complete will be the revulsion against false prophecy in that day that the natural bonds of family will not restrain the judgment.
13.4: When that day comes, the false prophets will be exposed and shamed — each one mortified by what he once claimed to have seen. They will abandon the prophet's rough cloak, the coarse wool garment they wore to look the part. The costume will no longer work, and they will know it.
13.5: The man will deny it outright: 'Prophet? No. I am a farmhand. I have been someone else's servant since I was a boy.' The prophetic office will become so discredited that men who once made their living at it will scramble to disown any connection. Better to be a hired laborer than to be identified with what prophecy has become.
13.6: Someone notices the marks and asks: 'What are those wounds on your hands?' He has a ready deflection: 'I got these at a friend's house.' The wounds he cannot explain away, and so he explains them as something ordinary. In their immediate context, these marks likely come from the discipline of 13:3 — or from some private violence he would rather not name. But the verse casts a long shadow: read from beyond the resurrection, the wounded hands of the stricken shepherd of 13:7 speak another word entirely.
13.7: Wake up, sword — strike my shepherd! Strike the man who stands beside me as my own equal, my intimate companion, declares Yahweh of Armies. Strike him down, and the flock scatters in every direction. Even the little ones will not escape my hand. Here is the incomprehensible mystery at the heart of redemption: Yahweh calls this shepherd his fellow — his equal, his associate — and then commands the sword against him. The disciples at Gethsemane are the scattered sheep; Jesus quotes this verse himself (Matthew 26:31, Mark 14:27). The stricken shepherd is the Son.
13.8: Yahweh declares it plainly: when the judgment sweeps through the land, two out of every three will be cut down and destroyed. Only one in three survives. The remnant is always a minority — but the remnant is real, and the remnant is the point.
13.9: That surviving third I will cast into the furnace. I will refine them the way silver is refined — burned through, every impurity drawn off. I will test them the way gold is tested — under full heat, nothing hidden. And then — the covenant formula restored at last: they will call my name, and I will answer. I will say: My people. They will say: Our God. This is the covenant language of Exodus and of Jeremiah's new covenant, made real through fire. The remnant that passes through the flames emerges as the people of God in a way nothing else could have produced.

14.1: Watch: the day of the LORD is approaching — and on that day, what has been stripped from Jerusalem will be divided up by the victors in plain sight of the city. This is not the end of the story. It is the turning point before the reversal.
14.2: It is the LORD himself who gathers the nations to converge on Jerusalem for war. The city will fall: houses ransacked, women violated, half the population marched away as captives. This is a scene of total devastation. But half remain. The remnant inside the walls has not been cut off — not yet. Something is about to change everything.
14.3: Then Yahweh himself strides out. He goes to war the way he has always gone to war — as the Divine Warrior, the one who fights for his people when all other hope is spent. The exodus. The Red Sea. Jericho. Now: Jerusalem.
14.4: He lands on the Mount of Olives — his feet touch down on the very ridge rising above the eastern gate of Jerusalem — and the mountain splits. A great valley tears open from east to west; the northern half slides north, the southern half south. The mountain that witnessed Christ's anguished prayer and his arrest becomes the platform of his return. Acts 1:11 promises he will come back the same way he left. The geography of redemption is not only symbolic — it is literal.
14.5: A path of escape opens through the newly cleft valley — flee through it, all the way to Azal. Flee the way your ancestors fled when the earthquake struck in Uzziah's reign; that earthquake was terrifying enough that its memory persisted for generations (Amos 1:1). What is coming makes even that look small. But the fleeing is not the final word. Following close behind the fleeing survivors: Yahweh my God, arriving with all his holy ones — his angelic retinue, the redeemed, or both. The King arrives with his full court.
14.6: On that day the normal order fails. The light will be neither brilliant nor deeply dark — a supernatural twilight that belongs to neither day nor night. Creation suspends its ordinary cycles. The familiar categories by which human beings have always measured time cease to apply.
14.7: It will be a day unlike any other — a day the LORD alone knows how to name, belonging neither to daytime nor to nighttime. And then, at what should be the hour of darkness, light breaks out instead. Evening becomes dawn. The darkness is reversed. The night becomes day. This is the same logic that governs resurrection: what should end in dark does not.
14.8: Living water — fresh, flowing, life-giving — will pour out from Jerusalem, splitting into two rivers: half flowing east toward the Dead Sea, half west toward the Mediterranean. Season-proof, drought-proof, permanent. Ezekiel 47 saw this river rising from the temple threshold. Jesus declared himself the source of the living water (John 7:37–38). Revelation 22 shows it flowing through the new Jerusalem. The river is always the same river.
14.9: On that day: Yahweh is king over every nation, every parcel of earth. And this is the theological crown — on that day Yahweh will be one and his name one. The fractured world in which many names, many gods, many competing claims divide human loyalty will come to an end. The Shema of Deuteronomy 6:4 will be true not only as Israel's confession but as universal reality. One God, acknowledged by all.
14.10: The surrounding landscape will be leveled — from Geba in the north to Rimmon in the south, the hills of Judah smoothed to flatness. But Jerusalem alone will rise: elevated above the plains, inhabited and undiminished. The ancient gates and towers the text names trace the full perimeter of the city — Benjamin's Gate, the Corner Gate, the Tower of Hananel, the royal winepresses. Jerusalem stands while everything around it bows.
14.11: People will make their home in Jerusalem again — and this time the herem, the ban of holy war, the decree of total destruction that devastated city after city through conquest and exile, will never fall on Jerusalem again. The city that suffered every assault, that was twice burned and its people twice deported, will finally rest in a safety that does not end.
14.12: Here is the judgment Yahweh brings on every nation that came against Jerusalem: a plague that does not wait for death. The flesh of the living begins to dissolve — while they are still standing upright, not yet fallen — bodies consuming from within. Eyes liquefy in their sockets. Tongues waste away in their mouths. This is the inverse of the fountain in 13:1: where one cleanses the repentant, the other corrupts the enemy. The violence they brought against God's city returns on their own bodies.
14.13: On that day Yahweh releases chaos through the enemy armies — a great panic, a divine confusion of the kind he unleashed on Sisera's forces and on the Midianites before Gideon. Soldiers who came as allies against Jerusalem find themselves gripping each other by the hand — not as comrades, but as enemies. The coalition dissolves into mutual slaughter. They came to destroy one city; they destroy each other instead.
14.14: Judah, too, enters the fight alongside Jerusalem. And in the aftermath, the wealth the nations brought to war flows back the other way: their gold, their silver, their garments and gear, collected in extraordinary abundance. What the nations came to take, they leave behind for the LORD's city.
14.15: The plague is not limited to soldiers. Every animal in the enemy camps — horses and mules, camels and donkeys, every beast that carried their weapons and their supplies — falls under the same judgment. The military apparatus of the nations is dismantled entirely: not only the men, but everything they depended on.
14.16: Those who survive from every nation that once marched against Jerusalem — they will become pilgrims. Year after year they will come up to Jerusalem to bow before the King, Yahweh of Armies, and to celebrate the Feast of Tabernacles. The nations that arrived as enemies become worshipers. The conquered city becomes the pilgrimage center of the world. The Feast of Tabernacles — the feast commemorating God's dwelling among his people in the wilderness — becomes the feast of his eternal presence among all nations.
14.17: Non-participation is not neutral. Any nation whose families refuse to make the pilgrimage to worship Yahweh the King will find their skies closed. No rain. The same covenant consequence that fell on Israel for disobedience now applies globally. Universal kingship carries universal accountability. There are no neutral bystanders in the eschatological order.
14.18: Egypt is named specifically — perhaps because Egypt relied on the Nile rather than rainfall, and might calculate it could ignore the requirement without consequence. But the LORD anticipates the loophole. Whether a nation depends on rain or river makes no difference; the plague will fall on Egypt just as it falls on every other holdout. There is no exemption through clever geography.
14.19: Egypt is the prominent example; all nations are the scope. The eschatological feast demands universal attendance. The consequences of refusal apply without exception to every holdout — no nation, however powerful or resourceful, stands outside the reach of the King.
14.20: The inscription that once marked the high priest's golden crown — HOLY TO THE LORD (Exodus 28:36) — will be stamped on the bridle bells of horses. The ordinary becomes sacred. The cooking pots in the LORD's house will be elevated to the same sanctity as the silver offering bowls used at the altar. The boundary between the common and the consecrated begins to dissolve. In the new order, holiness is not a restricted zone — it is the atmosphere of everything.
14.21: Every pot — every ordinary cooking vessel in Jerusalem and throughout Judah — will carry the sanctity that once belonged only to consecrated temple instruments. Any worshiper may take one, cook the sacrifice in it, and the act is wholly acceptable before God. Sacred and secular meet without friction. The book of Zechariah ends at its highest note: one final purge — no more traders in the house of Yahweh of Armies. The commercial element that had invaded the courts is gone for good. (Jesus's clearing of the temple in John 2:16 is a sign of this day; his words — 'my Father's house' — echo directly.) A world remade. A city consecrated. A God universally worshiped. All things holy.

7.1: Two years after the visions of chapters 1–6 — in the fourth year of Darius, on the fourth day of the ninth month, Chislev — the word of Yahweh came to Zechariah.
7.2: The community of Bethel had dispatched two envoys — Sharezer and Regemmelech — along with their delegation, to make supplication before Yahweh,
7.3: bearing a question for the priests of the temple of Yahweh of Armies and for the prophets: 'Should I continue the fast of the fifth month — the fast I have kept, year after year, through all these decades since Jerusalem fell?'
7.4: Yahweh of Armies had an answer. His word came to me:
7.5: 'Say this to all the people of the land and to the priests: For seventy years you have kept the fast of the fifth month and wept through the seventh. But was any of it really for me? Or was it, all along, for yourselves?'
7.6: Your feasts are for yourselves. Your fasts are for yourselves. Every ritual turned inward. I was never the audience.
7.7: What I am saying is not new. The former prophets said exactly this when Jerusalem was full and thriving, when the towns of the Negev and the Shephelah were alive with people. You had the same choice then.
7.8: The word of Yahweh came to Zechariah:
7.9: Yahweh of Armies said this: Judge honestly. Practice covenant loyalty and tender mercy toward each other.
7.10: Stop crushing the vulnerable — the widow, the orphan, the immigrant, the poor. Stop nursing schemes against your neighbor in the private quiet of your own heart.
7.11: But they would not listen. They pulled away, stiff-necked. They covered their ears against the word.
7.12: They turned their hearts to stone — harder than flint — so that the law could not penetrate, so that the words Yahweh's own Spirit had sent through the former prophets could find no entrance. And so: great wrath, from Yahweh of Armies himself.
7.13: They would not answer when I called. I did not answer when they cried. The symmetry is exact. Yahweh of Armies has spoken.
7.14: I swept them away — a whirlwind scattering them to nations they had never heard of. And the land they left behind fell silent. No one traveled through it. No one returned. The land they had called delightful lay empty and ruined. They had made it so.

8.1: The word of Yahweh of Armies came:
8.2: Yahweh of Armies says: I burn for Zion with the fire of a husband who will not release his claim. My jealousy is enormous. My fury on her behalf is fierce.
8.3: Yahweh says: I have come back to Zion. I will make my home in Jerusalem. And the city will carry the name it truly deserves: the faithful city. The mountain where I dwell will be called what it truly is: the holy mountain, the mountain of Yahweh of Armies.
8.4: Yahweh of Armies says: Old men and women, white-haired and unhurried, will sit in Jerusalem's streets again — staffs in hand, simply because they have lived long enough to need them.
8.5: And the streets that were empty will ring again with children's voices — boys and girls running and playing, unafraid.
8.6: Yahweh of Armies says: Yes — to the remnant, to people who have seen what they have seen, this sounds too good to be true. But is anything too difficult for me? The question hangs in the air. Yahweh of Armies has asked it.
8.7: Yahweh of Armies says: Watch. I am going to bring my people home — from the east, from the west, from every direction they were scattered.
8.8: I will bring them back. They will live in Jerusalem. And the covenant formula will be fulfilled at last — they will be my people and I will be their God — grounded not in aspiration but in faithfulness and right-standing before me.
8.9: Yahweh of Armies says: Take courage — every one of you listening to these words, spoken by the prophets who were there when the foundation of this house was laid. You are not starting from nothing. You are finishing something.
8.10: Before those days — you remember — no one earned enough. Neither worker nor beast saw a fair return. No one was safe stepping outside or coming home. I myself had set neighbor against neighbor. Those were the consequences of abandonment. They are finished.
8.11: But that is done. Yahweh of Armies declares it: I will not treat this remnant the way I treated those who came before them. The past does not repeat.
8.12: In place of the curse: a seed of wholeness planted in the ground. The vine will produce again. The earth will release what it had withheld. The sky will give its dew. And this remnant — these survivors — will receive it all as their inheritance.
8.13: You became a byword of disaster — 'May you end up like Judah and Israel.' That curse is about to reverse. I will save you. You will become the blessing — the nations will say, 'May you flourish as they do.' So: do not be afraid. Strengthen your hands.
8.14: Here is the logic Yahweh of Armies presents: When your ancestors provoked me, I set myself to bring calamity on them. I said it and I did not turn back.
8.15: By that same resolve — that same unyielding purpose — I have now determined to do good to Jerusalem and Judah. The very quality that made the judgment certain makes the promise sure. Do not be afraid.
8.16: And here is what you are to do in response: Tell each other the truth. In your courts, judge with integrity — judgments that are true and that build peace, not judgments that protect the powerful or settle old scores.
8.17: Stop nursing evil schemes against your neighbors in the quiet of your own heart. Stop taking false oaths. I hate all of this — Yahweh declares it — with a deep and settled hatred.
8.18: The word of Yahweh of Armies came to me:
8.19: Yahweh of Armies says: Those four fasts — the calendar of grief and commemoration — they will all become feasts. Gladness. Celebration. Joy. So love truth. Love peace. That is where the transformation begins.
8.20: Yahweh of Armies says: The day is coming. Peoples will come. Cities upon cities will send their residents on pilgrimage.
8.21: One city will call to another: 'Let's go — right now — to seek Yahweh's favor, to seek Yahweh of Armies. I am not waiting. I am going.' And they will go.
8.22: Nation after nation — mighty nations — will come to Jerusalem to seek Yahweh of Armies, to entreat his favor.
8.23: Yahweh of Armies says: When that day comes, ten men from every tongue on earth will grab the coat of one Jewish person and say: 'Take us with you. We have heard it — God is with you.' That is how this age ends.

9.1: An oracle: the word of Yahweh hangs heavy over the land of Hadrach. It will settle and rest on Damascus — for every human eye, and every eye in Israel, is fixed on Yahweh.
9.2: The word reaches Hamath on the border, and south to Tyre and Sidon — those shrewd cities, wise in their own estimation.
9.3: Tyre built herself into an island fortress. She stacked silver like dirt, piled gold like street mud. She thought wealth could be a wall.
9.4: But the Lord has seen it. He will seize what she hoarded and cast her power into the sea. Fire will finish what remains.
9.5: Ashkelon will watch and tremble. Gaza will see it and convulse. Ekron's confidence will collapse — all it had hoped for, gone. Gaza's king will vanish. Ashkelon will empty out.
9.6: A mongrel population will fill Ashdod. The ancestral pride of the Philistines will be sheared away.
9.7: I will take the blood-offerings from their mouths, the idol-food from between their teeth. And even this people — even the Philistines — can become part of what belongs to our God. Absorbed like a clan of Judah. Ekron will be like the Jebusites of old: once outside, then woven in.
9.8: And I will take my post around my own house — a guard of one, yet sufficient. No army will sweep through again. No oppressor will march over my people. I am watching now with my own eyes open.
9.9: Shout for joy, Daughter Zion! Cry it aloud, Jerusalem! Your king is on his way — and look at how he comes: just, and saved by God himself; humble, not on a warhorse but on a donkey's colt. This is what a true king looks like.
9.10: The instruments of war will be dismantled: chariots, warhorses, battle bows — all taken away. Not because peace was negotiated but because it was given. He will speak peace to every nation, and his reign will stretch from ocean to ocean, from the great River to the edges of the earth.
9.11: And for you, Jerusalem — for the sake of the covenant sealed in blood — I will release your prisoners from the dry pit where there is no water. The covenant holds. The blood speaks.
9.12: Come back — come back to the fortress, you who are captives but still carry hope. Yahweh declares it today: double will be restored. For everything taken, twice as much returned.
9.13: Judah is the bow I have bent. Ephraim is the arrow I have set to the string. Zion's sons I will rouse against the sons of Greece — and I will wield you like the sword of a warrior. You are my weapon.
9.14: Then Yahweh himself will appear above them — his arrow shooting like lightning from the sky. The Lord Yahweh will sound the ram's horn and march forward in the storm winds of the south. The divine warrior has entered the field.
9.15: Yahweh of Armies stands over them as a shield. They will surge forward — consuming, trampling — exultant as warriors flush with wine. They will be filled like the blood-bowls of the altar, saturated like the altar's corners at the great sacrifice.
9.16: On that day Yahweh their God will rescue them — the flock he has always owned. And they will be displayed over the land like gemstones set in a crown, catching the light for all to see.
9.17: What goodness — what beauty — belongs to Yahweh! Grain will give the young men strength to grow tall. New wine will bring the young women to joy. Life, flourishing, abundance — all from his hand.

10.1: Ask Yahweh for rain — ask in the season when rain is needed, when the spring rains are due. He is the one who builds the storm clouds. He will answer with showers, with green across every field.
10.2: But instead they have been going to the household idols — gods that speak only nonsense. The diviners see visions that are false, dream dreams that are lies, and give comfort that leaves everyone empty. And so the people drift, bewildered as sheep with no one to lead them — and they have suffered for it.
10.3: My anger burns against those who were supposed to shepherd — the leaders who fed themselves instead of the flock. I will deal with them. Because Yahweh of Armies has not abandoned Judah — they are still his flock — and he will transform them into a magnificent warhorse.
10.4: From Judah — from this very people — the cornerstone will come: the founding stone of what is built. From him the tent peg that holds the structure firm. From him the battle bow. From him every leader that matters. All that the nation needs rises from within.
10.5: Together they will be warriors — trampling enemies into the mud. They will fight, and they will win, because Yahweh is with them. Even the proud cavalry will be put to shame.
10.6: I will give Judah its strength back. I will rescue the house of Joseph. I will bring them home, because my compassion for them has not dried up. They will be restored as if the rejection had never happened — as if they had never been cast away. For I am Yahweh their God. And I will hear them when they call.
10.7: Ephraim will become a warrior nation again — hearts bursting with joy like a man who has drunk deeply. Their children will witness it and join the exultation. Their hearts will overflow in Yahweh.
10.8: I will whistle — one signal — and they will come. I have already redeemed them. They will multiply again until they are as numerous as they ever were.
10.9: I scattered them — yes — but even in the distant countries, even in exile, memory will hold them. They will remember me. They will raise children. And they will come back.
10.10: From Egypt I will bring them. From Assyria I will gather them. I will settle them in Gilead and Lebanon — until the land cannot hold them all.
10.11: He will march through the sea of anguish — striking the waves, parting the waters again as at the Exodus. The Nile's deepest channels will go dry. The pride of Assyria will be crushed to earth. Egypt's staff of power will be stripped away. The old oppressors will yield.
10.12: I will give them strength — and it will be strength rooted in Yahweh himself. They will live inside his name, move inside his name. Yahweh has declared it.

11.1: Lebanon — throw open your gates! Fire is coming for your cedars.
11.2: Cry, cypress trees — the cedar has fallen. Grieve what once was glorious and is now wreckage. Wail, oaks of Bashan — the whole thick forest has come down.
11.3: That sound is the shepherds wailing — their lush pastures, their pride, gone. That other sound is the lions roaring — the dense thickets of the Jordan, their home, stripped bare.
11.4: Yahweh my God said this to me: 'Take up the staff. Shepherd the flock. But know what you are walking into — this is the flock already marked for slaughter.'
11.5: The buyers butcher them and feel no guilt. The sellers pocket the profit and thank God for their wealth. And the ones who should have protected them — their very own shepherds — have not a drop of compassion.
11.6: Yahweh declares: I am withdrawing my protection from the people of this land. I will hand them over — neighbor to neighbor, subject to king. The land will be shattered. And I will not reach down to pull anyone out.
11.7: So I did it. I took up the shepherd's work for the flock heading to slaughter — I tended them for the sake of the merchants who had been exploiting them. I carried two staffs: I named one Grace and the other Unity. And I tended the flock.
11.8: In the space of a single month I removed three shepherds — three false leaders swept away. But by then my patience with the people was spent, and they had come to despise me.
11.9: So I said: I am done. I resign from shepherding you. What is dying — let it die. What is being destroyed — let it be destroyed. What is left — let them turn on each other and feed. I am finished.
11.10: I took the staff I had named Grace and snapped it in two. The covenant I had made with all the nations — to protect this people — was annulled.
11.11: It was broken that day. And the sheep-traders who had watched the whole performance suddenly understood: this was not theater. This was the word of Yahweh.
11.12: I asked them to settle accounts: 'If you think I have been worth anything, pay me. If not, never mind.' They weighed out thirty pieces of silver.
11.13: 'Throw it to the potter,' Yahweh said — and the irony in the command was like thunder: 'this magnificent sum they have assigned me.' So I took the thirty pieces of silver and hurled them toward the potter in the house of Yahweh. A price had been paid. A judgment had been rendered.
11.14: Then I snapped the second staff — Unity — and with it the bond of brotherhood between Judah and Israel was severed. Two peoples that had been held together: now apart.
11.15: Then Yahweh said: 'Take it up again — this time, the full equipment of a foolish shepherd. Play the role through to the end.'
11.16: Because what is coming is a shepherd raised up in the land who abandons the dying, ignores the wandering, neglects the broken, and leaves the healthy to fend for themselves — who instead strips the fat sheep down to their very hooves, feeding himself on the flock. The anti-shepherd. The one who takes instead of gives.
11.17: Judgment falls on the hollow, useless shepherd who abandons his flock. The sword will come for his arm — his strength will wither to nothing. The sword will come for his right eye — his sight will go dark. He will be stripped of the very things a shepherd needs.

12.1: An oracle: the word of Yahweh over Israel. This is the voice of the one who stretched the sky like a canopy, who set the earth on its foundation, who formed the human spirit and breathed it into the chest of every person born:
12.2: I am making Jerusalem into a cup of reeling — a vessel that sends the surrounding nations stumbling. And Judah itself will be swept into the siege of Jerusalem.
12.3: On that day Jerusalem becomes an immovable stone — all who try to move it will tear themselves open. Every nation under heaven will converge on it. Every one of them will be broken by it.
12.4: On that day — Yahweh declares it — every horse that comes will be seized with blind panic, every rider struck mad. But on the house of Judah my eyes will be open, watchful. And the horses of the nations: blind.
12.5: And the clan-leaders of Judah will say it in their own hearts: 'The people of Jerusalem are strong — not because of their walls, but because Yahweh of Armies, their God, stands with them.'
12.6: On that day I will make Judah's clans like a firepot dropped into a woodpile, like a burning torch thrown into a wheat field. They will burn through the surrounding nations — right, left — consuming. And all the while, Jerusalem will stand, inhabited, in her place.
12.7: Yahweh will rescue Judah's rural settlements first — ahead of Jerusalem — so that neither the house of David nor the city's population can look down on those outside the walls. The countryside matters to him.
12.8: On that day Yahweh will throw his shield over every person in Jerusalem. The most stumbling among them will fight like David. The house of David will be like a divine presence — like the angel of Yahweh going before the army. Weakness transformed. Smallness made great.
12.9: On that day I will make it my purpose — my sustained intention — to destroy every nation that has moved against Jerusalem.
12.10: Then I will pour out on the house of David and the people of Jerusalem a spirit of grace and earnest prayer. And when they look — when they truly look — at the one they have pierced, they will mourn. The mourning will be as deep as grief for an only child, as bitter as weeping for a firstborn son. The MT's pronoun shift — 'look on me... mourn for him' — is maintained: the pierced one and Yahweh speak in compressed, overlapping voice. The NT reads this as fulfilled in Christ (John 19:37; Rev 1:7).
12.11: On that day, Jerusalem's mourning will reach the depth of the grief of Megiddo — likely the lamentation over Josiah, slain there (2 Chr 35:24–25), a grief by which all other grief was measured.
12.12: The whole land will grieve — and the grief will be intimate, individual, not shared in crowd. The royal line of David: apart. Their wives: apart. The line of Nathan: apart. Their wives: apart.
12.13: The priestly line of Levi: apart. Their wives: apart. The family of Shimei: apart. Their wives: apart.
12.14: Every family that remains — each one alone, each wife in her own sorrow. The mourning is too large for crowds. It must be borne in private, face by face.
